From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Country in West Africa

This article is about the country. For the ancient empire, see [Ghana Empire](/wiki/Ghana_Empire "Ghana Empire"). For other uses, see [Ghana (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ghana_(disambiguation) "Ghana (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Gana](/wiki/Gana "Gana") or [Gaana](/wiki/Gaana "Gaana").

**Ghana**,[[a]](#cite_note-10) officially the **Republic of Ghana**, is a country in [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"). It is situated with the [Gulf of Guinea](/wiki/Gulf_of_Guinea "Gulf of Guinea") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the south, and shares borders with [Côte d'Ivoire](/wiki/C%C3%B4te_d%27Ivoire "Côte d'Ivoire") to the west, [Burkina Faso](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso") to the north, and [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo") to the east. Ghana covers an area of 239,567 km2 (92,497 sq mi), spanning diverse [ecologies](/wiki/Biome "Biome"), from [coastal](/wiki/Coast "Coast") [savannas](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") to [tropical rainforests](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"). With over 35 million inhabitants, Ghana is the [thirteenth-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_population "List of African countries by population") in Africa, and the second-most populous country in West Africa specifically. The [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and largest city is [Accra](/wiki/Accra "Accra").

The earliest kingdoms to emerge in Ghana were [Bonoman](/wiki/Bono_state "Bono state") in the south and the [Kingdom of Dagbon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Dagbon "Kingdom of Dagbon") in the north, with Bonoman existing in the area during the 11th century.[[10]](#cite_note-NP-11) The [Asante Empire](/wiki/Asante_Empire "Asante Empire") and other Akan kingdoms in the south emerged over the centuries.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Beginning in the 15th century, the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), followed by other [European powers](/wiki/European_Powers "European Powers"), contested the area for trading rights, until the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") ultimately established control of the coast by the 19th [century](/wiki/Century "Century"). Following more than a century of colonial resistance, the later borders of the country took shape, encompassing four separate British colonial territories: [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(British_colony) "Gold Coast (British colony)"), [Ashanti](/wiki/Ashanti_(Crown_Colony) "Ashanti (Crown Colony)"), the [Northern Territories](/wiki/Northern_Territories_of_the_Gold_Coast "Northern Territories of the Gold Coast"), and [British Togoland](/wiki/British_Togoland "British Togoland"). These were unified as an independent dominion within the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). On 6 March 1957 Ghana became the first [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony") in Sub-Saharan Africa to achieve sovereignty.[[12]](#cite_note-Universal_Newsreel-13)[[13]](#cite_note-First_For_Sub-Saharan_Africa-14)[[14]](#cite_note-Exploring_Africa_–_Decolonization-15) Under President [Kwame Nkrumah](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah "Kwame Nkrumah"), it became influential in [decolonisation efforts](/wiki/Decolonisation_of_Africa "Decolonisation of Africa") and the [Pan-African movement](/wiki/Pan-Africanism "Pan-Africanism").[[15]](#cite_note-Ateku-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Ghana is a [multi-ethnic](/wiki/Multi-ethnic "Multi-ethnic") country with diverse linguistic and religious groups;[[17]](#cite_note-popest-18) while the [Akan](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") are the largest ethnic group, they constitute a plurality. Most [Ghanaians](/wiki/Ghanaian_people "Ghanaian people") are [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Ghana "Christianity in Ghana") (71.3%); almost a fifth are [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Ghana "Islam in Ghana"); a tenth practise traditional faiths or report no religion.[[4]](#cite_note-statsghana1-4) Ghana is a unitary [constitutional democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") led by a [president](/wiki/President_of_Ghana "President of Ghana") who is [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[18]](#cite_note-Ghana:_CIA_World_FactBook-19) For political stability in Africa, Ghana ranked seventh in the 2022 [Ibrahim Index of African Governance](/wiki/Ibrahim_Index_of_African_Governance "Ibrahim Index of African Governance") and fifth in the 2024 [Fragile States Index](/wiki/Fragile_States_Index "Fragile States Index").[[15]](#cite_note-Ateku-16)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Ghana is a founding member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Ghana_and_the_Non-Aligned_Movement "Ghana and the Non-Aligned Movement") and the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), and a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [South Atlantic Peace and Cooperation Zone](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Peace_and_Cooperation_Zone "South Atlantic Peace and Cooperation Zone"), [Economic Community of West African States](/wiki/Economic_Community_of_West_African_States "Economic Community of West African States"), the [Group of 24](/wiki/Group_of_24 "Group of 24") and the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[20]](#cite_note-21)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

### Empire of Ghana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Empire of Ghana")]

Further information: [Ghana Empire](/wiki/Ghana_Empire "Ghana Empire")

The name *Ghana* comes from [Wagadu](/wiki/Ghana_Empire "Ghana Empire"), an empire in west Africa from the 3rd to 12th centuries; Wagadu was termed *Ghana* by Arab traders involved in the [trans-Saharan trade](/wiki/Trans-Saharan_trade "Trans-Saharan trade"). *Ghana* is thought to originate from the title *[Kaya Maghan](/wiki/Kaya-Magha "Kaya-Magha")* of the rulers of Wagadu, which translates as *ruler of gold*.[[21]](#cite_note-Gestrich-22)

### Adoption in precolonial scholarship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Adoption in precolonial scholarship")]

The earliest attempt to associate the peoples of the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(British_colony) "Gold Coast (British colony)") with ancient Ghana had been made by Rev. J. B. Anaman around the turn of the twentieth century, who drew on D. W. Cooley's nineteenth-century interpretation of Arabic geographical sources to make an argument for a historical connection, he suggested an alternative derivation connecting the name to the Wangara. [[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKimble1963xvi–xvii-23) [Lady Flora Shaw](/wiki/Flora_Shaw,_Baroness_Lugard "Flora Shaw, Baroness Lugard") later compiled both Arabic and European writings to create detailed narratives of the kingdom. She presented it as a major African power comparable to contemporary Western European states.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKimble1963xvi-24)

According to [Jack Goody](/wiki/Jack_Goody "Jack Goody"), the theory that the [Akan peoples](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") originated from the medieval [Empire of Ghana](/wiki/Ghana_Empire "Ghana Empire") was continuously promoted through the teachings of Rev. W. T. Balmer between 1907 and 1911, who instructed students that the Akan had migrated from the ancient kingdom located near the [Upper Niger](/wiki/Niger_River "Niger River").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1968461–462-25) Goody states that Balmer's hypothesis lacked linguistic and historical evidence, yet it later became influential among educated elites and nationalist intellectuals.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1968462–463-26)

The hypothesis gained more popularity when it was introduced into educational institutions, primarily in [Achimota](/wiki/Achimota_School "Achimota School"), during the 1920s where it later spread to other schools.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKimble1963xvii-27) The theory was later publicized by [J. B. Danquah](/wiki/J._B._Danquah "J. B. Danquah")'s academic writings; he used [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") translations to claim that the [Akan](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") migrated from the Upper Niger region. Danquah proposed that the term Ghana was a corruption of Akane or Akana and associated it with the ancient region of [Akkad](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1968468-28) Eva L. R. Meyerowitz expanded this rhetoric through a series of publications that sought to reconstruct early history. She claimed that Akan origins and culture came from areas in the Sahara and the Near East, and argued that Akan culture was not mainly black African, but could instead be considered [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya")-[Berber](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers") or connected to [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") or [Near Eastern](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") cultures.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1968468–470-29)

### National acceptance and symbolic meaning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: National acceptance and symbolic meaning")]

By the period leading to independence, the name "Ghana" was accepted and adopted as a symbol of precolonial prestige, cultural unity, and national legitimacy.[[21]](#cite_note-Gestrich-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Ghana](/wiki/History_of_Ghana "History of Ghana")

For a topical guide, see [Outline of Ghana](/wiki/Outline_of_Ghana "Outline of Ghana").

### Medieval kingdoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Medieval kingdoms")]

Main articles: [Ashanti Empire](/wiki/Ashanti_Empire "Ashanti Empire"), [Kingdom of Dagbon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Dagbon "Kingdom of Dagbon"), and [Bono state](/wiki/Bono_state "Bono state")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Guinea_from_Milner%27s_Atlas.jpg/250px-Guinea_from_Milner%27s_Atlas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guinea_from_Milner%27s_Atlas.jpg)

An 1850 map showing the [Akan](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") [Kingdom of Ashanti](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ashanti "Kingdom of Ashanti") within the [Guinea region](/wiki/Guinea_(region) "Guinea (region)") and surrounding regions in West Africa

The earliest kingdoms to emerge in Ghana were [Bonoman](/wiki/Bono_state "Bono state") in the south and the [Kingdom of Dagbon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Dagbon "Kingdom of Dagbon") in the north, with Bonoman existing in the area during the 11th century.[[10]](#cite_note-NP-11)[[29]](#cite_note-col-30) From the 17th century, different Akan states began to emerge from what is believed to have been the Bonoman area, mainly based on gold trading.[[30]](#cite_note-31) These states included Bonoman (Brong-Ahafo region), [Adansi](/wiki/Adansi "Adansi") and [Asante](/wiki/Asante_Empire "Asante Empire") ([Ashanti Region](/wiki/Ashanti_Region "Ashanti Region")), [Denkyira](/wiki/Denkyira "Denkyira") ([Western North region](/wiki/Central_Region_(Ghana) "Central Region (Ghana)")), [Mankessim Kingdom](/wiki/Mankessim_Kingdom "Mankessim Kingdom") ([Central region](/wiki/Western_Region_(Ghana) "Western Region (Ghana)")), [Akyem](/wiki/Akyem "Akyem") and [Akwamu](/wiki/Akwamu "Akwamu") [(Eastern region)](/wiki/Eastern_Region_(Ghana) "Eastern Region (Ghana)").[[29]](#cite_note-col-30)[[31]](#cite_note-32) By the 19th century, the territory of the southern part of Ghana was included in the [Asante Kingdom](/wiki/Ashanti_Empire "Ashanti Empire").[[29]](#cite_note-col-30) The government of the Ashanti Empire operated first as a loose network and eventually as a centralised kingdom with a specialised bureaucracy centred in the capital city of [Kumasi](/wiki/Kumasi "Kumasi").[[29]](#cite_note-col-30) Prior to Akan contact with Europeans, the Akan people created an economy based on principally gold and [gold bar](/wiki/Gold_bar "Gold bar") precious metals, which were traded with other states in Africa.[[29]](#cite_note-col-30)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The Ga-Dangme migrated westward from south-western [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"). The Ewe, formerly known as Dogbo, migrated from Ketu (Benin) area with their Gbe-speaking kinsmen (Adja, Fon, Phla/Phera and Ogun/Gun) and, in transition, settled at Tado in Togo, and Dogbo Nyigbo in Benin Republic, with Nortsie (a walled town in present-day Togo) as their final dispersal point. Their dispersal from Nortsie was necessitated by the high-handed rule of King Agorkorli (Agɔ Akɔli), who was the reigning monarch of the tribe at that time. The Ewe in Ghana speak three principal dialects: Anlo (along the coast), Tongu (along the Volta river) and Ewedome (in the hill country side). The [Ga-Dangme](/wiki/Ga-Adangbe_people "Ga-Adangbe people") occupy the Greater Accra Region and parts of the Eastern Region, while the Ewe are found in the [Volta Region](/wiki/Volta_Region "Volta Region") as well as the neighbouring Togo, Benin Republic and Nigeria (around Badagry area).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[33]](#cite_note-34)

### European contact and colonialism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: European contact and colonialism")]

See also: [Slave Coast of West Africa](/wiki/Slave_Coast_of_West_Africa "Slave Coast of West Africa") and [Dutch Slave Coast](/wiki/Dutch_Slave_Coast "Dutch Slave Coast")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Elmina_Castle_-_Ghana.jpg/250px-Elmina_Castle_-_Ghana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elmina_Castle_-_Ghana.jpg)

The [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") established the [Portuguese Gold Coast](/wiki/Portuguese_Gold_Coast "Portuguese Gold Coast") with the construction of [Elmina Castle](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle") (*Castelo da Mina*) by [Diogo de Azambuja](/wiki/Diogo_de_Azambuja "Diogo de Azambuja") in 1482, making it the oldest [European building](/wiki/Portuguese_colonial_architecture "Portuguese colonial architecture") in West Africa.

Akan trade with European states began after contact with the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") in the 15th century.[[34]](#cite_note-35) European contact was by the [Portuguese people](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people"), who came to the [Gold Coast region](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(region) "Gold Coast (region)") in the 15th century to trade. The Portuguese then established the [Portuguese Gold Coast](/wiki/Portuguese_Gold_Coast "Portuguese Gold Coast") (Costa do Ouro), focused on the availability of gold.[[35]](#cite_note-History-36) The Portuguese built a trading lodge at a coastal settlement called Anomansah (the perpetual drink), which they renamed [São Jorge da Mina](/wiki/Elmina "Elmina").[[35]](#cite_note-History-36) In 1481, King [John II of Portugal](/wiki/John_II_of_Portugal "John II of Portugal") commissioned [Diogo de Azambuja](/wiki/Diogo_de_Azambuja "Diogo de Azambuja") to build the [Elmina Castle](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle"), which was completed in three years.[[35]](#cite_note-History-36) By 1598, the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") had joined the Portuguese in the gold trade, establishing the [Dutch Gold Coast](/wiki/Dutch_Gold_Coast "Dutch Gold Coast") (*Nederlandse Bezittingen ter Kuste van Guinea* – 'Dutch properties at the Guinea coast') and building forts at [Fort Komenda](/wiki/Fort_Komenda "Fort Komenda") and Kormantsi.[[36]](#cite_note-Ghana_book-37) In 1617, the Dutch captured the Elmina Castle from the Portuguese and [Axim](/wiki/Axim "Axim") in 1642 ([Fort St Anthony](/wiki/Fort_Saint_Anthony "Fort Saint Anthony")).[[36]](#cite_note-Ghana_book-37)

European traders had joined in gold trading by the 17th century, including the [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes "Swedes"), establishing the [Swedish Gold Coast](/wiki/Swedish_Gold_Coast "Swedish Gold Coast") (*Svenska Guldkusten*), and [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"), establishing the [Danish Gold Coast](/wiki/Danish_Gold_Coast "Danish Gold Coast") (*Danske Guldkyst* or *Dansk Guinea*).[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38) European traders participated in the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") in this area.[[38]](#cite_note-Emmer-39) More than 30 forts and castles were built by the merchants. The Germans established the [Brandenburger Gold Coast](/wiki/Brandenburger_Gold_Coast "Brandenburger Gold Coast") or Groß Friedrichsburg.[[39]](#cite_note-40) In 1874, Great Britain established control over some parts of the country, assigning these areas the status of the [British Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(British_colony) "Gold Coast (British colony)").[[40]](#cite_note-colestablish-41) [Military](/wiki/Military "Military") engagements occurred between British colonial powers and Akan nation-states. The Kingdom of [Ashanti](/wiki/Ashanti_Empire "Ashanti Empire") defeated the British some times in the 100-year-long [Anglo-Ashanti wars](/wiki/Anglo-Ashanti_wars "Anglo-Ashanti wars") and eventually lost with the [War of the Golden Stool](/wiki/War_of_the_Golden_Stool "War of the Golden Stool") in 1900.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-Chronology_of_world_history-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Transition to independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transition to independence")]

See also: [Dominion of Ghana](/wiki/Dominion_of_Ghana "Dominion of Ghana") and [Ghana Independence Act 1957](/wiki/Ghana_Independence_Act_1957 "Ghana Independence Act 1957")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Ghana_Independence_overprint_on_Gold_Coast_1s_stamp_1957.jpg/250px-Ghana_Independence_overprint_on_Gold_Coast_1s_stamp_1957.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Independence_overprint_on_Gold_Coast_1s_stamp_1957.jpg)

A Gold Coast postage stamp overprinted for Ghanaian independence in 1957

Celebrations marking Ghana's independence on 6 March 1957

In 1947, the newly formed [United Gold Coast Convention](/wiki/United_Gold_Coast_Convention "United Gold Coast Convention") led by ["The Big Six"](/wiki/The_Big_Six_(Ghana) "The Big Six (Ghana)") called for "self-government within the shortest possible time" following the [1946 Gold Coast legislative election](/wiki/1946_Gold_Coast_general_election "1946 Gold Coast general election").[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38)[[44]](#cite_note-45) [Kwame Nkrumah](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah "Kwame Nkrumah"), a Ghanaian nationalist who led Ghana from 1957 to 1966 as the country's first [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ghana "Prime Minister of Ghana") and [president](/wiki/President_of_Ghana "President of Ghana"), formed the [Convention People's Party](/wiki/Convention_People%27s_Party "Convention People's Party") in 1949 with the motto "self-government now".[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38) The party initiated a "positive action" campaign involving non-violent protests, strikes and non-cooperation with the British authorities. Nkrumah was arrested and sentenced to one year imprisonment during this time. In the Gold Coast's [1951 general election](/wiki/1951_Gold_Coast_general_election "1951 Gold Coast general election"), he was elected to Parliament and was released from prison.[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38)

At midnight on 6 March 1957, the Gold Coast, Ashanti, the Northern Territories, and [British Togoland](/wiki/British_Togoland "British Togoland") were unified as one single independent dominion within the [British Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") under the name Ghana. This was done under the [Ghana Independence Act 1957](/wiki/Ghana_Independence_Act_1957 "Ghana Independence Act 1957"). The current [flag of Ghana](/wiki/Flag_of_Ghana "Flag of Ghana"), consisting of the colours red, gold, green, and a black star, dates back to this unification.[[45]](#cite_note-Ghana_flag_and_description-46) On 1 July 1960, following the [Ghanaian constitutional referendum](/wiki/1960_Ghanaian_constitutional_referendum "1960 Ghanaian constitutional referendum") and [Ghanaian presidential election](/wiki/1960_Ghanaian_presidential_election "1960 Ghanaian presidential election"), Nkrumah declared Ghana a republic and assumed the presidency.[[12]](#cite_note-Universal_Newsreel-13)[[13]](#cite_note-First_For_Sub-Saharan_Africa-14)[[14]](#cite_note-Exploring_Africa_–_Decolonization-15)[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38) 6 March is the nation's [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Ghana) "Independence Day (Ghana)"), and 1 July is celebrated as [Republic Day](/wiki/Republic_Day "Republic Day").[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

[Nkrumah](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah "Kwame Nkrumah") led an [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") regime in Ghana, as he repressed political opposition and conducted elections that were not free and fair.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) In 1964, a [constitutional amendment](/wiki/1964_Ghanaian_constitutional_referendum "1964 Ghanaian constitutional referendum") made Ghana a [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state"), with Nkrumah as [president for life](/wiki/President_for_life "President for life") of both the nation and its party.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Nkrumah was the first African head of state to promote the concept of [Pan-Africanism](/wiki/Pan-Africanism "Pan-Africanism"), which he had been introduced to during his studies at [Lincoln University](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(Pennsylvania) "Lincoln University (Pennsylvania)"), Pennsylvania in the United States, at the time when [Marcus Garvey](/wiki/Marcus_Garvey "Marcus Garvey") was known for his "Back to Africa Movement".[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38) He merged the teachings of Garvey, [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") and the naturalised Ghanaian scholar [W. E. B. Du Bois](/wiki/W._E._B._Du_Bois "W. E. B. Du Bois") into the formation of 1960s Ghana.[[37]](#cite_note-History_of_Ghana-38) Osagyefo Dr. Kwame Nkrumah, as he became known, played an instrumental part in the founding of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), and in establishing the [Kwame Nkrumah Ideological Institute](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah_Ideological_Institute "Kwame Nkrumah Ideological Institute") to teach his ideologies of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism").[[54]](#cite_note-55) His life achievements were recognised by Ghanaians during his centenary birthday celebration, and the day was instituted as a [public holiday in Ghana](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Ghana "Public holidays in Ghana") ([Founders' Day](/wiki/Founders%27_Day_(Ghana) "Founders' Day (Ghana)")).[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Operation Cold Chop and aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Operation Cold Chop and aftermath")]

Main article: [History of Ghana (1966–1979)](/wiki/History_of_Ghana_(1966%E2%80%931979) "History of Ghana (1966–1979)")

The government of Nkrumah was subsequently overthrown in a coup by the [Ghana Armed Forces](/wiki/Ghana_Armed_Forces "Ghana Armed Forces"), codenamed "Operation Cold Chop". This occurred while Nkrumah was abroad with [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") in the People's Republic of China, on a mission to [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"), Vietnam, to help end the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). The coup took place on 24 February 1966, led by Colonel [Emmanuel Kwasi Kotoka](/wiki/Emmanuel_Kwasi_Kotoka "Emmanuel Kwasi Kotoka") and Brigadier Akwasi Afrifa. The [National Liberation Council](/wiki/National_Liberation_Council "National Liberation Council") was formed, chaired by Lieutenant General [Joseph A. Ankrah](/wiki/Joseph_Arthur_Ankrah "Joseph Arthur Ankrah").[[56]](#cite_note-political_&_social_thought_of_Kwame_Nkrumah-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)

A series of alternating military and civilian governments, often affected by economic instabilities,[[58]](#cite_note-59) ruled Ghana from 1966, ending with the ascent to power of [Flight Lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_lieutenant "Flight lieutenant") [Jerry John Rawlings](/wiki/Jerry_Rawlings "Jerry Rawlings") of the [Provisional National Defence Council](/wiki/Provisional_National_Defence_Council "Provisional National Defence Council") in 1981.[[59]](#cite_note-Flight_Lieutenant_Jerry_John_Rawlings_(J.J_Rawlings)-60) These changes resulted in the suspension of the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ghana "Constitution of Ghana") in 1981 and the banning of [political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Ghana "List of political parties in Ghana").[[60]](#cite_note-Rawlings:_The_legacy-61) The economy soon declined, so Rawlings negotiated a structural adjustment plan, changing many old economic policies, and growth recovered during the mid-1980s.[[60]](#cite_note-Rawlings:_The_legacy-61) A new constitution restoring [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") politics was promulgated in the [presidential election of 1992](/wiki/1992_Ghanaian_presidential_election "1992 Ghanaian presidential election"), in which Rawlings was elected, and again in the [general election of 1996](/wiki/1996_Ghanaian_general_election "1996 Ghanaian general election").[[61]](#cite_note-Elections_in_Ghana-62)

In a [tribal war](/wiki/Konkomba%E2%80%93Nanumba_conflict "Konkomba–Nanumba conflict") in [Northern Ghana](/wiki/Northern_Region_(Ghana) "Northern Region (Ghana)") in 1994, between the [Konkomba](/wiki/Konkomba_people "Konkomba people") and other ethnic groups, including the [Nanumba](/wiki/Nanumba_people "Nanumba people"), [Dagomba](/wiki/Dagomba_people "Dagomba people") and [Gonja](/wiki/Gonja_people "Gonja people"), between 1,000 and 2,000 people were killed and 150,000 people were displaced.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Kpetoee.jpg/250px-Kpetoee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kpetoee.jpg)

Traditional [chiefs](/wiki/Chieftaincy "Chieftaincy") in 2015

After the [2000 general election](/wiki/2000_Ghanaian_general_election "2000 Ghanaian general election"), [John Kufuor](/wiki/John_Kufuor "John Kufuor") of the [New Patriotic Party](/wiki/New_Patriotic_Party "New Patriotic Party") became president of Ghana on 7 January 2001 and was [re-elected in 2004](/wiki/2004_Ghanaian_general_election "2004 Ghanaian general election"), thus also serving two terms (the term limit) as president of Ghana and marking the first time under the fourth republic that power was transferred from one legitimately elected head of state and head of government to another.[[61]](#cite_note-Elections_in_Ghana-62)

[Nana Akufo-Addo](/wiki/Nana_Akufo-Addo "Nana Akufo-Addo"), the ruling party candidate, was defeated in a very close [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_Ghanaian_general_election "2008 Ghanaian general election") by [John Atta Mills](/wiki/John_Atta_Mills "John Atta Mills") of the [National Democratic Congress](/wiki/National_Democratic_Congress_(Ghana) "National Democratic Congress (Ghana)").[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) Mills died of natural causes and was succeeded by Vice President [John Mahama](/wiki/John_Mahama "John Mahama") on 24 July 2012.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Following the [2012 general election](/wiki/2012_Ghanaian_general_election "2012 Ghanaian general election"), Mahama became president in his own right,[[66]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_President_John_Dramani_Mahama_sworn_in-67) and Ghana was described as a "stable democracy".[[67]](#cite_note-OBG2012-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69) As a result of the [2016 general election](/wiki/2016_Ghanaian_general_election "2016 Ghanaian general election"),[[69]](#cite_note-70) Nana Akufo-Addo became president on 7 January 2017.[[70]](#cite_note-71) He was re-elected after a tightly contested [election in 2020](/wiki/2020_Ghanaian_general_election "2020 Ghanaian general election").[[71]](#cite_note-72)

To combat deforestation, on 11 June 2021, Ghana inaugurated Green Ghana Day, with the aim of planting five million trees.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main articles: [Geography of Ghana](/wiki/Geography_of_Ghana "Geography of Ghana") and [Geology of Ghana](/wiki/Geology_of_Ghana "Geology of Ghana")

See also: [Biodiversity of Ghana](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_Ghana "Biodiversity of Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Ghana_Topography.png/250px-Ghana_Topography.png)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Topography.png)

[Topographic map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Ghana_sat.png/250px-Ghana_sat.png)](/wiki/File:Ghana_sat.png)

[Satellite image](/wiki/Satellite_imagery "Satellite imagery")

Ghana lies on the [Gulf of Guinea](/wiki/Gulf_of_Guinea "Gulf of Guinea") in [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), a few degrees north of the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). It covers 238,540 km2 (92,101 sq mi) and has an [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") coastline of about 560 kilometres (350 miles).[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74) It is situated between latitudes 4°45′N and 11°N, and longitudes 1°15′E and 3°15′W. The [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian") passes through [Tema](/wiki/Tema "Tema"), near Accra, making Ghana the country closest to the intersection of the Prime Meridian and the Equator (0°, 0°), located offshore in the Atlantic.[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74)

Grasslands mixed with south coastal shrublands and forests dominate Ghana, with forest extending northward from the coast 320 kilometres (200 miles) and eastward for a maximum of about 270 kilometres (170 miles) with locations for mining of industrial minerals and timber.[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74) Ghana is home to five terrestrial ecoregions: [Eastern Guinean forests](/wiki/Eastern_Guinean_forests "Eastern Guinean forests"), [Guinean forest–savanna mosaic](/wiki/Guinean_forest%E2%80%93savanna_mosaic "Guinean forest–savanna mosaic"), [West Sudanian savanna](/wiki/West_Sudanian_savanna "West Sudanian savanna"), [Central African mangroves](/wiki/Central_African_mangroves "Central African mangroves"), and [Guinean mangroves](/wiki/Guinean_mangroves "Guinean mangroves").[[74]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-75)

The [White Volta](/wiki/White_Volta "White Volta") River and its tributary [Black Volta](/wiki/Black_Volta "Black Volta"), flow south through Ghana to [Lake Volta](/wiki/Lake_Volta "Lake Volta"), the world's [third-largest reservoir by volume](/wiki/List_of_reservoirs_by_volume "List of reservoirs by volume") and largest by surface area, formed by the hydroelectric [Akosombo Dam](/wiki/Akosombo_Dam "Akosombo Dam"),[[75]](#cite_note-76) completed in 1965.[[76]](#cite_note-auto2-77) The Volta flows out of Lake Volta into the [Gulf of Guinea](/wiki/Gulf_of_Guinea "Gulf of Guinea").[[77]](#cite_note-auto1-78) The northernmost part of Ghana is Pulmakong and the southernmost part of Ghana is [Cape Three Points](/wiki/Cape_Three_Points "Cape Three Points").[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Ghana_Regions_map.png/500px-Ghana_Regions_map.png)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Regions_map.png)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main articles: [Climate of Ghana](/wiki/Climate_of_Ghana "Climate of Ghana") and [Climate change in Ghana](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Ghana "Climate change in Ghana")

The [climate of Ghana](/wiki/Climate_of_Ghana "Climate of Ghana") is [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate"), and there is [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") and [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season").[[78]](#cite_note-UNDP_Climate-79) Ghana sits at the intersection of three hydro-climatic zones.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The [eastern coastal belt](/wiki/Dahomey_Gap "Dahomey Gap") is warm and comparatively dry, the south-west corner of Ghana is hot and [humid](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"), and the north of Ghana is hot and dry.[[80]](#cite_note-Ghana_high_plains-81)

[Climate change in Ghana](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Ghana "Climate change in Ghana") is having significant impacts on the people of Ghana. Increasing temperatures and changes in [rainfall](/wiki/Rainfall "Rainfall"), [extreme weather](/wiki/Extreme_weather "Extreme weather"), [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), [wild fires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood") and [sea-level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise")[[81]](#cite_note-82) are expected to negatively affect the country's infrastructure, hydropower production, [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security"), [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply"), and coastal and agricultural [livelihoods](/wiki/Livelihood "Livelihood") such as farming and fisheries.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-climatelinks-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85) Ghana's economy will be impacted by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), due to its dependence on [climate-sensitive](/wiki/Climate_sensitivity "Climate sensitivity") sectors such as [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), and [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"). Diseases like [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), [dengue fever](/wiki/Dengue_fever "Dengue fever") and [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") are predicted to increase due to changes in water conditions.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Ghana signed the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") in 2016. Ghana aims to avoid 64 million metric tons of [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") by 2030, compared to a business-as-usual scenario for 2020–2030. The country has committed to [net zero](/wiki/Net-zero_emissions "Net-zero emissions") by 2060.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### Relief and regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Relief and regions")]

The country consists of low coastal plains, forested hills in the centre, and savanna in the north. Its main geographical units are:

* the [Coastal Plain](/wiki/Coastal_Plain "Coastal Plain") along the Gulf of Guinea,
* the [Ashanti uplands](/w/index.php?title=Ashanti_uplands&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ashanti uplands (page does not exist)") in the centre,
* the [Volta Basin](/wiki/Volta_Basin "Volta Basin") in the east, dominated by [Lake Volta](/wiki/Lake_Volta "Lake Volta"),
* the [Northern Plains](/wiki/Northern_Plains "Northern Plains") stretching to Burkina Faso.[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74)

The highest point is [Mount Afadja](/wiki/Mount_Afadja "Mount Afadja") (885 m) in the Akwapim-Togo Range.[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74)

### Hydrology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hydrology")]

The [Volta River](/wiki/Volta_River "Volta River") system dominates Ghana. Its main tributaries, the [White Volta](/wiki/White_Volta "White Volta") and [Black Volta](/wiki/Black_Volta "Black Volta"), converge in the north and flow into [Lake Volta](/wiki/Lake_Volta "Lake Volta"), one of the world's largest artificial reservoirs, created by the [Akosombo Dam](/wiki/Akosombo_Dam "Akosombo Dam") in 1965.[[76]](#cite_note-auto2-77)

The Volta exits into the Gulf of Guinea east of Accra. Other important rivers include the [Pra River](/wiki/Pra_River_(Ghana) "Pra River (Ghana)"), [Ankobra River](/wiki/Ankobra_River "Ankobra River"), and [Tano River](/wiki/Tano_River "Tano River").[[77]](#cite_note-auto1-78)

### Extremities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Extremities")]

The northernmost settlement in Ghana is Pulmakong near the Burkina Faso border, while the southernmost point is [Cape Three Points](/wiki/Cape_Three_Points "Cape Three Points").[[73]](#cite_note-Geography_Physical-74)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main articles: [Government of Ghana](/wiki/Government_of_Ghana "Government of Ghana") and [Politics of Ghana](/wiki/Politics_of_Ghana "Politics of Ghana")

See also: [Regions of Ghana](/wiki/Regions_of_Ghana "Regions of Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Ghana_Parliament_House_%E2%80%93_Ghana_Supreme_Court_%E2%80%93_Osu_Castle.JPG/250px-Ghana_Parliament_House_%E2%80%93_Ghana_Supreme_Court_%E2%80%93_Osu_Castle.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Parliament_House_%E2%80%93_Ghana_Supreme_Court_%E2%80%93_Osu_Castle.JPG)

[Parliament House of Ghana](/wiki/Parliament_House_of_Ghana "Parliament House of Ghana"), the Supreme Court of Ghana, and [Judiciary of Ghana](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Ghana "Judiciary of Ghana") buildings. [Jubilee House](/wiki/Jubilee_House "Jubilee House") is the [presidential palace](/wiki/Presidential_palace "Presidential palace").

Ghana is a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_executive_theory "Unitary executive theory") [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [constitutional democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") with a parliamentary [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") that is dominated by two parties—the [National Democratic Congress (NDC)](/wiki/National_Democratic_Congress_(Ghana) "National Democratic Congress (Ghana)") and the [New Patriotic Party (NPP)](/wiki/New_Patriotic_Party "New Patriotic Party"). Ghana alternated between civilian and military governments until January 1993, when the military government gave way to the Fourth Republic of Ghana after [presidential](/wiki/1992_Ghanaian_presidential_election "1992 Ghanaian presidential election") and [parliamentary elections](/wiki/1992_Ghanaian_parliamentary_election "1992 Ghanaian parliamentary election") in late 1992. The 1992 [constitution of Ghana](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ghana "Constitution of Ghana") divides powers among a [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Ghana Armed Forces](/wiki/Ghana_Armed_Forces "Ghana Armed Forces") ([President of Ghana](/wiki/President_of_Ghana "President of Ghana")), parliament ([Parliament of Ghana](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ghana "Parliament of Ghana")), cabinet ([Cabinet of Ghana](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Ghana "Cabinet of Ghana")), council of state ([Ghanaian Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Ghana) "Council of State (Ghana)")), and an independent judiciary ([Judiciary of Ghana](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Ghana "Judiciary of Ghana")). The government is elected by [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") after every four years.[[87]](#cite_note-cs-88)

The 2012 [Fragile States Index](/wiki/Fragile_States_Index "Fragile States Index") indicated that Ghana is ranked the 67th-least fragile state in the world and the fifth-least fragile state in Africa. Ghana ranked 112th out of 177 countries on the index.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Ghana ranked as the 64th-least corrupt and politically corrupt country in the world out of all 174 countries ranked and ranked as the fifth-least politically corrupt country in Africa out of 53 countries in the 2012 [Transparency International Corruption Perception Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index").[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91) Ghana was ranked seventh in Africa out of 53 countries in the 2012 [Ibrahim Index of African Governance](/wiki/Ibrahim_Index_of_African_Governance "Ibrahim Index of African Governance"). The Ibrahim Index is a comprehensive measure of African government, based on variables which reflect the success with which governments deliver essential political goods to its citizens.[[91]](#cite_note-92) According to 2023 [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") Ghana is ranked 67th electoral democracy worldwide and 10th [electoral democracy in Africa](/wiki/Democracy_in_Africa "Democracy in Africa").[[92]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-93)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of Ghana](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Ghana "Foreign relations of Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Kofi_Annan_at_OYW.jpg/250px-Kofi_Annan_at_OYW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kofi_Annan_at_OYW.jpg)

[Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), Ghanaian diplomat and United Nations Secretary-General 1997–2006

Since independence, Ghana has been devoted to ideals of nonalignment and is a founding member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"). Ghana favours international and regional political and economic co-operation, and is an active member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union").[[93]](#cite_note-H._E._Mr._Ken_Kanda-94)

Three U.S. presidents have made diplomatic trips to Ghana ([Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")), along with a Vice President ([Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris")).[[94]](#cite_note-95) Some Ghanaian diplomats and politicians hold positions in international organisations, including Ghanaian diplomat and former [Secretary-General of the United Nations](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations") [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") Judge [Akua Kuenyehia](/wiki/Akua_Kuenyehia "Akua Kuenyehia"), and former President [Jerry John Rawlings](/wiki/Jerry_Rawlings "Jerry Rawlings") and former President [John Agyekum Kufuor](/wiki/John_Kufuor "John Kufuor"), who both served as diplomats of the United Nations.[[87]](#cite_note-cs-88)

In September 2010, President [John Atta Mills](/wiki/John_Atta_Mills "John Atta Mills") visited China on an official visit. Mills and then-[general secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") marked the 50th anniversary of [diplomatic ties](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Ghana_relations "China–Ghana relations") between the two nations, at the [Great Hall of the People](/wiki/Great_Hall_of_the_People "Great Hall of the People").[[95]](#cite_note-Hu_Jintao-John_Atta_Mills-96) China reciprocated with an official visit in November 2011, by the vice-chairman of the [Standing Committee of the National People's Congress of China](/wiki/Standing_Committee_of_the_National_People%27s_Congress "Standing Committee of the National People's Congress"), [Zhou Tienong](/wiki/Zhou_Tienong "Zhou Tienong") who visited Ghana and met with Ghana's president [John Mahama](/wiki/John_Mahama "John Mahama").[[96]](#cite_note-Xinhua2011-97) China became one of the top investing countries of Ghana, which predominantly focus on infrastructure, natural resources, and the [manufacturing sector](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy"), have promoted economic growth, job creation, and technology transfer in Ghana. Concerns regarding the sustainability of Chinese-financed projects, environmental impacts, and the lack of transparency in their investments call for a careful assessment of these collaborations.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Iranian President [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/wiki/Mahmoud_Ahmadinejad "Mahmoud Ahmadinejad") met with Mahama in 2013 to hold discussions on strengthening the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") and also co–chair a bilateral meeting between Ghana and Iran at the Ghanaian [presidential palace](/wiki/Presidential_palace "Presidential palace") [Flagstaff House](/wiki/Jubilee_House "Jubilee House").[[98]](#cite_note-Ahmadinejad-99)[[99]](#cite_note-Ahmadinejad2-100)[[100]](#cite_note-CPP_welcomes_President_Ahmadinejad_visit_to_Ghana-101)[[101]](#cite_note-Ghana_welcomed_Iran's_President_Mahmoud_Ahmadinejad-102)[[102]](#cite_note-President_Mahmoud_Ahmadinejad-103)

The [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals_and_Ghana "Sustainable Development Goals and Ghana") (SDG) were integrated into Ghana's development agenda and the budget. According to reports, the SDGs were implemented through a decentralized planning approach. This allows for stakeholders' participation, such as in UN agencies, traditional leaders, civil society organizations, academia, and others.[[103]](#cite_note-104) The 17 SDGs are a global call to action to end poverty among others, and the UN and its partners in the country are working towards achieving them.[[104]](#cite_note-105) According to the President [Nana Akufo-Addo](/wiki/Nana_Akufo-Addo "Nana Akufo-Addo"), Ghana was "the first Sub-Saharan African country to achieve the goal of halving poverty, as contained in Goal 1 of the [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals")".[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Ghana Armed Forces](/wiki/Ghana_Armed_Forces "Ghana Armed Forces")

In 1957, the [Ghana Armed Forces](/wiki/Ghana_Armed_Forces "Ghana Armed Forces") (GAF) consisted of its headquarters, support services, three battalions of infantry and a reconnaissance squadron with armoured vehicles.[[106]](#cite_note-Canada's_Military_Assistance-107) President Nkrumah aimed at rapidly expanding the GAF to support the [United States of Africa](/wiki/United_States_of_Africa "United States of Africa") ambitions. Thus, in 1961, 4th and 5th Battalions were established, and in 1964 6th Battalion was established, from a [parachute](/wiki/Paratrooper "Paratrooper") [airborne unit](/wiki/Airborne_forces "Airborne forces") originally raised in 1963.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Today, Ghana is a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") and [regional hegemon](/wiki/Regional_hegemony "Regional hegemony").[[108]](#cite_note-South_America_and_West_Africa-109) In his book *[Shake Hands with the Devil](/wiki/Shake_Hands_with_the_Devil_(book) "Shake Hands with the Devil (book)")*, [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces") commander [Roméo Dallaire](/wiki/Rom%C3%A9o_Dallaire "Roméo Dallaire") highly rated the GAF soldiers and military personnel.[[106]](#cite_note-Canada's_Military_Assistance-107)

The [military operations](/wiki/Military_operation "Military operation") and [military doctrine](/wiki/Military_doctrine "Military doctrine") of the GAF are conceptualised in the constitution, Ghana's Law on Armed Force Military Strategy, and [Kofi Annan International Peacekeeping Training Centre](/wiki/Kofi_Annan_International_Peacekeeping_Training_Centre "Kofi Annan International Peacekeeping Training Centre") agreements to which GAF is attestator.[[109]](#cite_note-Defence-110)[[110]](#cite_note-Ghana's_Regional_Security_Policy-111)[[111]](#cite_note-KAIPTC-112) GAF military operations are executed under the auspices and imperium of the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Ghana) "Ministry of Defence (Ghana)").[[109]](#cite_note-Defence-110)[[112]](#cite_note-Vision_&_Mission-113) Ghana has experienced political violence in the past and 2017 has thus far seen an upward trend in incidents motivated by political grievances.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

### Law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Law enforcement")]

Further information: [Law enforcement in Ghana](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Ghana "Law enforcement in Ghana") and [Crime in Ghana](/wiki/Crime_in_Ghana "Crime in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Ghana_Police_Service_Motorbikes.jpg/250px-Ghana_Police_Service_Motorbikes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Police_Service_Motorbikes.jpg)

[Militarized police](/wiki/Militarized_police "Militarized police") Unit of the [Ghana Police Service](/wiki/Ghana_Police_Service "Ghana Police Service")

The [Ghana Police Service](/wiki/Ghana_Police_Service "Ghana Police Service") and the [Criminal Investigation Department](/wiki/Criminal_Investigation_Department "Criminal Investigation Department") are the main law enforcement agencies, responsible for the detection of crime, maintenance of law and order and the maintenance of internal peace and security.[[114]](#cite_note-MINT-115) The Ghana Police Service has eleven specialised police units, including a [Militarized police](/wiki/Militarized_police "Militarized police") [Rapid deployment force](/wiki/Rapid_deployment_force "Rapid deployment force") and [Marine Police Unit](/wiki/Ghana_Police_Service#Marine_Police_Unit "Ghana Police Service").[[115]](#cite_note-UNIT-116)[[116]](#cite_note-POLICE-117) The Ghana Police Service operates in 12 divisions: ten covering the regions of Ghana, one assigned specifically to the seaport and industrial hub of [Tema](/wiki/Tema "Tema"), and the twelfth being the Railways, Ports and Harbours Division.[[116]](#cite_note-POLICE-117) The Ghana Police Service's Marine Police Unit and Division handles issues that arise from the country's offshore [oil and gas industry](/wiki/Oil_and_gas_industry "Oil and gas industry").[[116]](#cite_note-POLICE-117)

The [Ghana Prisons Service](/wiki/Ghana_Prisons_Service "Ghana Prisons Service") and the sub-division [Borstal Institute for Juveniles](/wiki/Borstal_Institute_for_Juveniles "Borstal Institute for Juveniles") administers incarceration.[[117]](#cite_note-Ghana_Prisons_Service_General_Information-118) The new sustainable development goals adopted by the United Nations call for the international community to come together to promote the rule of law; support equal access to justice for all; reduce corruption; and develop effective, accountable, and transparent institutions at all levels.[[118]](#cite_note-DIPNote:_Promoting_Peace,_Justice,_and_Strong_Institutions_in_the_Great_Lakes-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Drugtrade.png/250px-Drugtrade.png)](/wiki/File:Drugtrade.png)

Ghana is among the sovereign states of West Africa used by [drug cartels](/wiki/Drug_cartel "Drug cartel") and drug traffickers (shown in orange).

Ghana is used as a key narcotics industry transshipment point by traffickers, usually from South America and some from other African nations.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120) In 2013, the UN chief of the Office on Drugs and Crime stated that "West Africa is completely weak in terms of border control and the big drug cartels from [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") have chosen Africa as a way to reach Europe."[[120]](#cite_note-DW_Made_for_minds:_Illegal_drug_use_on_the_rise_in_Africa-121) The social context within which narcotic trafficking, storage, transportation, and repacking systems exist in Ghana and the state's location along the Gulf of Guinea makes Ghana a more attractive country for the narcotics business.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Ghana_could_be_taken_over_by_drug_barons_if-122) The [Narcotics Control Board](/wiki/Narcotics_Control_Board_(Ghana) "Narcotics Control Board (Ghana)") has impounded container ships at the Sekondi Naval Base in the [Takoradi Harbour](/wiki/Takoradi_Harbour "Takoradi Harbour"). These ships were carrying thousands of kilograms of cocaine, with a street value running into billions of [Ghana cedis](/wiki/Ghanaian_cedi "Ghanaian cedi"). Drug seizures saw a decline in 2011.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Ghana_could_be_taken_over_by_drug_barons_if-122) [Drug cartels](/wiki/Drug_cartel "Drug cartel") are using new methods in narcotics production and narcotics exportation, to avoid Ghanaian security agencies.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Ghana_could_be_taken_over_by_drug_barons_if-122) Underdeveloped institutions, porous open borders, and the existence of established smuggling organisations contribute to Ghana's position in the narcotics industry.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Ghana_could_be_taken_over_by_drug_barons_if-122) President Mills initiated ongoing efforts to reduce the role of airports in Ghana's drug trade.[[119]](#cite_note-Ghana_hit_by_illegal_drug_trade-120)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Human rights")]

See also: [Human rights in Ghana](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Ghana "Human rights in Ghana") and [LGBT rights in Ghana](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Ghana "LGBT rights in Ghana")

[Homosexual acts](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") are prohibited by law in Ghana.[[122]](#cite_note-123) According to a 2013 survey by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 96% of Ghanaians believe that homosexuality should not be accepted by society.[[123]](#cite_note-pewglobal.org-124) Sometimes elderly [women in Ghana](/wiki/Women_in_Ghana "Women in Ghana") are accused of [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft"), particularly in rural Ghana. Issues of witchcraft mainly remain as speculations based on superstitions within families. In some parts of northern Ghana, there exist what are called [witch camps](/wiki/Witch_camp "Witch camp"). These are said to house a total of around 1,000 people accused of witchcraft.[[124]](#cite_note-Camps-125) The Ghanaian government has announced that it intends to close the camps.[[124]](#cite_note-Camps-125)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Ghana](/wiki/Economy_of_Ghana "Economy of Ghana")

See also: [Agriculture in Ghana](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Ghana "Agriculture in Ghana") and [Manufacturing in Ghana § Automobile manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_Ghana#Automobile_manufacturing "Manufacturing in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/GDP_per_capita_development_development_of_Ghana.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_development_of_Ghana.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_development_of_Ghana.svg)

Change in per capita GDP, 1870–2018. Figures are inflation-adjusted to 2011 International dollars.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Ghana_Export_Trends.jpg/250px-Ghana_Export_Trends.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Export_Trends.jpg)

Ghana's [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), gold, and [cocoa](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean"); exports in [percentage](/wiki/Percentage "Percentage")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

Ghana possesses [industrial minerals](/wiki/Industrial_mineral "Industrial mineral"), [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") and [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"). It is an emerging designated [digital economy](/wiki/Digital_economy "Digital economy") with [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") hybridisation and an [emerging market](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market"). It has an economic plan target known as the "Ghana Vision 2020". This plan envisions Ghana as the first African country to become a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") between 2020 and 2029 and a [newly industrialised country](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_country "Newly industrialized country") between 2030 and 2039.[[125]](#cite_note-126) This excludes fellow [Group of 24](/wiki/Group_of_24 "Group of 24") member and Sub-Saharan African country [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), which is a newly industrialised country.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

Ghana's economy has ties to the [Chinese yuan renminbi](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") along with Ghana's gold reserves. In 2013, the [Bank of Ghana](/wiki/Bank_of_Ghana "Bank of Ghana") began circulating the renminbi throughout Ghanaian state-owned banks and to the Ghana public as [hard currency](/wiki/Hard_currency "Hard currency") along with the national [Ghanaian cedi](/wiki/Ghanaian_cedi "Ghanaian cedi") for second national trade currency.[[127]](#cite_note-BoG_introduce_Chinese_Yuan_onto_the_FX_market-128)

Between 2012 and 2013, 38% of rural dwellers were experiencing poverty whereas 11% of urban dwellers were.[[128]](#cite_note-129) Urban areas hold greater opportunity for employment, particularly in informal trade, while 94% of "rural poor households" participate in the agricultural sector.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

The [Volta River Authority](/wiki/Volta_River_Authority "Volta River Authority") and the [Ghana National Petroleum Corporation](/wiki/Ghana_National_Petroleum_Corporation "Ghana National Petroleum Corporation"), both state-owned, are the two major electricity producers.[[130]](#cite_note-Ghana_–_Gross_Domestic_Product-131) The [Akosombo Dam](/wiki/Akosombo_Dam "Akosombo Dam"), built on the Volta River in 1965, along with the [Bui Dam](/wiki/Bui_Dam "Bui Dam"), the [Kpong Dam](/wiki/Kpong_Dam "Kpong Dam") and other hydroelectric dams, provide [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower").[[131]](#cite_note-132)[[132]](#cite_note-New_fuel_for_faster_development-133)

The [Ghana Stock Exchange](/wiki/Ghana_Stock_Exchange "Ghana Stock Exchange") is the fifth largest on continental Africa and 3rd largest in Sub-Saharan Africa with a [market capitalisation](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of [GH¢](/wiki/Ghana_Cedi "Ghana Cedi") 57.2 billion or [CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi")180.4 billion in 2012 with the South Africa [JSE Limited](/wiki/JSE_Limited "JSE Limited") as first.[[133]](#cite_note-134) The Ghana Stock Exchange was the second best performing [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") in Sub-Saharan Africa in 2013.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Ghana produces [cocoa](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean").[[135]](#cite_note-Ghana_Entering_A_Sweet,_Golden_Era-136) It is the second largest producer of cocoa globally and its [ICCO](/wiki/International_Cocoa_Organization "International Cocoa Organization") membership helps in its international cocoa trade.[[136]](#cite_note-137) Ghana is classified as a middle-income country.[[5]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.GH-5)[[137]](#cite_note-138) [Services](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") account for 50% of GDP, followed by manufacturing (24.1%), [extractive industries](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy") (5%), and taxes (20.9%).[[130]](#cite_note-Ghana_–_Gross_Domestic_Product-131)

Ghana's economy is characterized by a growing manufacturing sector and the export of digital technology products. The country is also engaged in the assembly and export of automobiles and ships. Ghana's economy benefits from a range of resource-rich exports, including industrial minerals and agricultural products, with cocoa being a primary commodity. The nation is a producer and exporter of [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas").[[138]](#cite_note-Ghana's_Jubilee_oil_field_nears_output_plateau_-operator-139)

The [information and communications technology](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology") (ICT) sector plays a role in Ghana's industrial landscape, with companies such as [Rlg Communications](/wiki/Rlg_Communications "Rlg Communications"), a state-affiliated digital technology corporation, leading in the production of tablet computers, smartphones, and consumer electronics.[[139]](#cite_note-The_Top_5_Countries_for_ICT_in_Africa-140)

Urban [electric cars](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") have been manufactured in Ghana since 2014.[[140]](#cite_note-141)[[141]](#cite_note-142)

Ghana announced plans to issue government debt by way of social and green bonds in autumn of 2021, making it the first African country to do so.[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144) The country, which was planning to borrow up to $5 billion in international markets,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] would use the proceeds from these sustainable bonds to refinance debt used for social and environmental projects and pay for educational or health. The country will use the proceeds to forge ahead with a free secondary-school initiative started in 2017 among other programs, while having recorded its lowest economic growth rate in 37 years in 2020.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Jubilee_Oil_Field_of_the_Ghana_National_Petroleum_Corporation_%28GNPC%29_and_National_Petroleum_Authority.png/250px-Jubilee_Oil_Field_of_the_Ghana_National_Petroleum_Corporation_%28GNPC%29_and_National_Petroleum_Authority.png)](/wiki/File:Jubilee_Oil_Field_of_the_Ghana_National_Petroleum_Corporation_(GNPC)_and_National_Petroleum_Authority.png)

[Jubilee Oil Field](/wiki/Jubilee_Oil_Field "Jubilee Oil Field") of the [Ghana National Petroleum Corporation](/wiki/Ghana_National_Petroleum_Corporation "Ghana National Petroleum Corporation") and [National Petroleum Authority](/wiki/National_Petroleum_Authority "National Petroleum Authority"), located off the coast of the [Western Region](/wiki/Western_Region_(Ghana) "Western Region (Ghana)")

Ghana produces and exports [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") such as [sweet crude oil](/wiki/Sweet_crude_oil "Sweet crude oil") and natural gas.[[145]](#cite_note-Five_Countries_to_Watch-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147) The 100%-state-owned [filling station](/wiki/Filling_station "Filling station") company, [Ghana Oil Company](/wiki/Ghana_Oil_Company "Ghana Oil Company"), is the number one petroleum and gas filling station, and the 100%-state-owned state oil company Ghana National Petroleum Corporation oversees [hydrocarbon exploration](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_exploration "Hydrocarbon exploration") and production of petroleum and natural gas reserves. Ghana aims to further increase the output of oil to 2.2 million barrels (350,000 m3) per day and gas to 34,000,000 cubic metres (1.2×10~~^~~9 cu ft) per day.[[147]](#cite_note-cs2-148) The [Jubilee Oil Field](/wiki/Jubilee_Oil_Field "Jubilee Oil Field"), which contains up to 3 billion barrels (480,000,000 m3) of sweet crude oil, was discovered in 2007.[[148]](#cite_note-149) Ghana is believed to have up to 5 billion barrels (790,000,000 m3) to 7 billion barrels (1.1×109 m3) of petroleum in reserves,[[149]](#cite_note-150) which is the fifth-largest in Africa and the 21st-to-25th-[largest proven reserves](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_proven_oil_reserves "List of countries by proven oil reserves") in the world. It also has up to 1.7×1011 cubic metres (6×10~~^~~12 cu ft) of natural gas in reserves.[[150]](#cite_note-Atuabo_gas_project_to_propel_more_growth-151) The government has drawn up plans to [nationalise](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") petroleum and natural gas reserves to increase government revenue.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

In 2015, Ghana produced 88 metric tonnes of gold as per the *Our World in Data* report.[[152]](#cite_note-153) As of 2019, Ghana was the 7th largest producer of gold in the world, producing ~140 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne") that year.[[153]](#cite_note-CEIC-154) This record saw Ghana surpass South Africa in output for the first time, making Ghana the largest gold producer in Africa.[[154]](#cite_note-155) In addition to gold, Ghana exports [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), timber, [diamonds](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond"), [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite"), and [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), and has other mineral deposits.[[155]](#cite_note-Ghana_Mineral_and_Mining_Sector_Investment_and_Business_Guide-156) Ghana ranks 9th in the world in diamond export and reserve size.[[156]](#cite_note-157) The government has drawn up plans to [nationalize](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") mining industry to increase government revenue.[[155]](#cite_note-Ghana_Mineral_and_Mining_Sector_Investment_and_Business_Guide-156)[[157]](#cite_note-158)

"Shortages" of electricity in 2015 and 2016 led to [dumsor](/wiki/Dumsor "Dumsor") ("persistent, irregular and unpredictable" electric power outages),[[158]](#cite_note-germany-159) increasing the interest in renewables.[[159]](#cite_note-graphic1-160) As of 2019, there is a surplus of electricity.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

The [judicial system of Ghana](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Ghana "Judiciary of Ghana") deals with corruption, economic malpractice and lack of economic transparency. According to Transparency International's [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") of 2018, out of 180 countries, Ghana was ranked 78th, with a score of 41 on a scale where a 0–9 score means highly corrupt, and a 90–100 score means very clean. This was based on perceived levels of public sector corruption.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

### Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Ghana launched a cellular mobile network in 1992. It was later connected to the Internet and introduced [ADSL](/wiki/ADSL "ADSL") broadband services.[[162]](#cite_note-Ghanaweb-163)

Ghana was ranked 101st in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)

The [Ghana Space Science and Technology Centre](/wiki/Ghana_Space_Science_and_Technology_Centre "Ghana Space Science and Technology Centre") (GSSTC) and Ghana Space Agency (GhsA) oversee [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration") and space programmes. GSSTC and GhsA worked to have a [national security](/wiki/National_security "National security") [observational satellite](/wiki/Earth_observation_satellite "Earth observation satellite") launched into orbit in 2015.[[165]](#cite_note-BBCSpace-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167) Ghana's annual space exploration expenditure has been 1% of its GDP, to support research in science and technology. In 2012, Ghana was elected to chair the [Commission on Science and Technology for Sustainable Development in the South](/wiki/Commission_on_Science_and_Technology_for_Sustainable_Development_in_the_South "Commission on Science and Technology for Sustainable Development in the South") (Comsats); Ghana has a joint effort in space exploration with the [South African National Space Agency](/wiki/South_African_National_Space_Agency "South African National Space Agency").[[165]](#cite_note-BBCSpace-166)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Ghana](/wiki/Tourism_in_Ghana "Tourism in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Surfers_Surfing_at_Busua_Beach_in_Western_region%2C_Ghana.jpg/250px-Surfers_Surfing_at_Busua_Beach_in_Western_region%2C_Ghana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surfers_Surfing_at_Busua_Beach_in_Western_region,_Ghana.jpg)

[Surfers](/wiki/Surfer "Surfer") at [Busua Beach](/wiki/Busua_Beach "Busua Beach") in the Western Region[[167]](#cite_note-11_of_the_world's_most_unusual_surf_spots-168)

In 2011, tourists visiting Ghana numbered 1,087,000,[[168]](#cite_note-WAO-169) with arrivals including South Americans, Asians, Europeans, and North Americans.[[169]](#cite_note-Trade_Expo_International_Ghana-170) Among the attractions and tourist destinations are waterfalls such as [Kintampo waterfalls](/wiki/Kintampo_waterfalls "Kintampo waterfalls") and the largest waterfall in west Africa, [Wli waterfalls](/wiki/Wli_waterfalls "Wli waterfalls"), the coastal palm-lined sandy beaches, caves, mountains, rivers, and reservoirs and lakes such as [Lake Bosumtwi](/wiki/Lake_Bosumtwi "Lake Bosumtwi") and the largest human-made lake in the world by surface area, [Lake Volta](/wiki/Lake_Volta "Lake Volta"), dozens of [forts and castles](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_Ghana "List of castles in Ghana"), [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), nature reserves and national parks.[[169]](#cite_note-Trade_Expo_International_Ghana-170) Castles include [Cape Coast Castle](/wiki/Cape_Coast_Castle_Museum "Cape Coast Castle Museum") and the [Elmina Castle](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle").[[170]](#cite_note-171) Castles mark where blood was shed in the slave trade and preserve and promote the African heritage stolen and destroyed through the slave trade.[[171]](#cite_note-Centre-172) The World Heritage Convention of UNESCO named Ghana's castles and forts as World Heritage Monuments: "The Castles and Forts of Ghana shaped not only Ghana's history but that of the world over four centuries as the focus of first the gold trade and then the slave trade. They are a significant and emotive symbol of European–African encounters and of the starting point of the African Diaspora."[[171]](#cite_note-Centre-172)

The [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") statistics in 2010 showed that out of the world's favourite tourist destinations, Ghana was ranked 108th out of 139 countries.[[172]](#cite_note-RANK-173) The country had moved two places up from the 2009 rankings. In 2011, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* magazine published that Ghana was ranked the 11th most friendly country in the world. The assertion was based on a survey in 2010 of a cross-section of travellers. Of all the African countries that were included in the survey, Ghana ranked highest.[[172]](#cite_note-RANK-173) Tourism is the fourth highest earner of foreign exchange for the country.[[172]](#cite_note-RANK-173) In 2024, Ghana ranked as the [55th most peaceful country](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index") in the world.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

Up and down the coastline, surfing spots have been identified and cultivated by locals and internationals. Surfers have made trips to the country to sample the waves. Surfers carried their boards amid [traditional fishing vessels](/wiki/Traditional_fishing_boat "Traditional fishing boat").[[174]](#cite_note-175)

According to Destination Pride,[[175]](#cite_note-176) Ghana's Pride score is 22 (out of 100).[[176]](#cite_note-177)

Main article: [Demographics of Ghana](/wiki/Demographics_of_Ghana "Demographics of Ghana")

Further information: [Ghanaian people](/wiki/Ghanaian_people "Ghanaian people")

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit), the United Nations reports Ghana has a population of 34,581,288.[[177]](#cite_note-GH-178) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit), around 29% of the population is under the age of 15, while persons aged 15–64 make up 57.8% of the population.[[178]](#cite_note-databank.worldbank.org-179) The 2010 census reported that the largest ethnic groups are the [Akan](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") (47.3%), the [Mole-Dagbani](/wiki/Mole-Dagbon_people "Mole-Dagbon people") (18.5%), the [Ewe](/wiki/Ewe_people "Ewe people") (13.9%), the [Ga-Dangme](/wiki/Ga-Dangme_people "Ga-Dangme people") (7.4%), the [Gurma](/wiki/Gurma_people "Gurma people") (5.7%) and the [Guan](/wiki/Guang_people "Guang people") (3.7%).[[179]](#cite_note-statsghana.gov.gh-180) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit), the United Nations reports the median age of Ghanaian citizens is 21 years old.[[180]](#cite_note-181) Ghana contributes 0.42% to the total world population.[[177]](#cite_note-GH-178)

With [recent legal immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Ghana "Immigration to Ghana") of [skilled workers](/wiki/Skilled_worker "Skilled worker") who possess [Ghana Cards](/wiki/Ghana_Card "Ghana Card"), there is a small but increasing population of Chinese, Malaysian, Indian, Middle Eastern, American and European nationals and their descendants born in Ghana. In 2010, the [Ghana Immigration Service](/wiki/Ghana_Immigration_Service "Ghana Immigration Service") reported many [economic migrants](/wiki/Economic_migrant "Economic migrant") and [undocumented immigrants inhabiting Ghana](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_Ghana "Illegal immigration to Ghana"): 14.6% (or 3.1 million) of Ghana's 2010 population (mostly Nigerians, Burkinabe citizens, Togolese citizens, and Malian citizens). In 1969, under the "Ghana Aliens Compliance Order" enacted by then Prime Minister [Kofi Abrefa Busia](/wiki/Kofi_Abrefa_Busia "Kofi Abrefa Busia"),[[181]](#cite_note-Ghana_Owes_no_Apology_to_Anybody_for_Aliens_Compliance_Order-182) the [Border Guard Unit](/wiki/Border_Guard_Unit "Border Guard Unit") deported more than 3,000,000 aliens and undocumented immigrants in three months as they made up 20% of the population at the time.[[181]](#cite_note-Ghana_Owes_no_Apology_to_Anybody_for_Aliens_Compliance_Order-182)[[182]](#cite_note-The_History_of_Ghana's_1969_Aliens_Compliance_Order-183)[[183]](#cite_note-184) In 2013, there was a mass deportation of undocumented miners, more than 4,000 of whom were Chinese nationals.[[184]](#cite_note-185)[[185]](#cite_note-186)
