### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of Ghana](/wiki/Languages_of_Ghana "Languages of Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Predominant_tribe_in_the_area_-_%28Ghana%29_LOC_88692692.jpg/330px-Predominant_tribe_in_the_area_-_%28Ghana%29_LOC_88692692.jpg)](/wiki/File:Predominant_tribe_in_the_area_-_(Ghana)_LOC_88692692.jpg)

Map of Ghana's ethno-linguistic areas

English is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of Ghana.[[186]](#cite_note-USA-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188) Additionally, there are eleven languages that have the status of government-sponsored languages:

* [Akan languages](/wiki/Akan_languages "Akan languages") ([Asante Twi](/wiki/Asante_dialect "Asante dialect"), [Akuapem Twi](/wiki/Akuapem_Twi "Akuapem Twi"), [Fante](/wiki/Fante_dialect "Fante dialect"), [Bono](/wiki/Bono_language "Bono language") which have a high degree of mutual intelligibility, and [Nzema](/wiki/Nzema_language "Nzema language"), which is less intelligible with the above)
* [Dangme](/wiki/Dangme_language "Dangme language")
* [Ewe](/wiki/Ewe_language "Ewe language")
* [Ga](/wiki/Ga_language "Ga language")
* [Guan](/wiki/Gua_language "Gua language")
* [Kasem](/wiki/Kasena_language "Kasena language")
* [Mole–Dagbani languages](/wiki/Mole%E2%80%93Dagbani_languages "Mole–Dagbani languages") ([Dagaare](/wiki/Dagaare "Dagaare") and [Dagbanli](/wiki/Dagbani_language "Dagbani language"))[[188]](#cite_note-NCA-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190)

Of these, Asante Twi is the most widely spoken.[[190]](#cite_note-Introduction_To_The_Verbal_and_Multi-Verbalsystem_of_Akan-191)

Because Ghana is surrounded by [French-speaking countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_French_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where French is an official language"), French is widely taught in schools and used for commercial and international economic exchanges. Hausa is also widely spoken in the northern part of Ghana especially among the Muslim communities. Since 2005, Ghana has been an associate member of the *[Organisation internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie")*,[[191]](#cite_note-192) the global organisation that unites French-speaking countries (84 nations on six continents). In 2005, more than 350,000 Ghanaian children studied French in schools. Since then, its status has been progressively updated to a mandatory language in every junior high school,[[192]](#cite_note-193) and it is in the process of becoming an official language.[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-195)

[Ghanaian Pidgin English](/wiki/Ghanaian_Pidgin_English "Ghanaian Pidgin English"), also known as Kru English (or in Akan, *kroo brofo*), is a variety of [West African Pidgin English](/wiki/West_African_Pidgin_English "West African Pidgin English") spoken in Accra and in the southern towns.[[195]](#cite_note-Huber_1999_p139-196) It can be divided into two varieties, referred to as "uneducated" or "non-institutionalized" pidgin and "educated" or "institutionalized" pidgin, the former associated with uneducated or illiterate people and the latter acquired and used in institutions such as universities.[[196]](#cite_note-Huber_1999_p138-153-197)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Ghana](/wiki/Religion_in_Ghana "Religion in Ghana")

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the largest religion in Ghana, with 71.3% of the population being members of various [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") as of the 2021 census.[[197]](#cite_note-198) [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is practiced by 16.8% of the total population. According to a 2012 report by [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research "Pew Research"), 60.71% of Muslims are followers of [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), while around 9.52% identify with [Shia](/wiki/Shia "Shia") Islam, while the remainder 29.76% are [non-denominational Muslims](/wiki/Non-denominational_Muslim "Non-denominational Muslim").[[198]](#cite_note-TheWorld'sMuslims:UnityandDiversity-199)[[199]](#cite_note-rs-200) There is "no significant link between ethnicity and religion in Ghana". Approximately 16% belong to the [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") religion. Rest 8.7% are others.[[200]](#cite_note-stte.gov-201)

Ghana has around 150,000 [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses").[[201]](#cite_note-202)

The [Grand Orange Lodge of Ghana](/wiki/Orange_Order_in_Africa "Orange Order in Africa") is a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") [fraternal organization](/wiki/Fraternity "Fraternity") within the [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order").[[202]](#cite_note-203) The most famous governed by the [Grand Lodge](/wiki/Grand_Lodge "Grand Lodge") is The Loyal Leopold Lodge No. 907. It was founded in 1894 by a British [colonial](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") official, Dr. A. D. MacDonald. The lodge initially served as a [social club](/wiki/Social_club "Social club") for European administrators, [military officers](/wiki/Military_officers "Military officers"), and merchants.[[203]](#cite_note-204) Over time, it began to admit local African members, which was a significant departure from the lodges in Ireland and Britain that were overwhelmingly white and Protestant. Today, its members continue to participate in events and [parades](/wiki/Parade "Parade"), including [The Twelfth](/wiki/The_Twelfth "The Twelfth").[[204]](#cite_note-205) They also are involved in community and charitable work.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

### Universal health care and life expectancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Universal health care and life expectancy")]

Main article: [Health in Ghana](/wiki/Health_in_Ghana "Health in Ghana")

Further information: [Eye care in Ghana](/wiki/Eye_care_in_Ghana "Eye care in Ghana") and [Optometry in Ghana](/wiki/Optometry_in_Ghana "Optometry in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Life_expectancy_in_Ghana.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_Ghana.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_Ghana.svg)

Development of life expectancy, 1921 to 2019

Ghana has a [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system, [National Health Insurance Scheme](/wiki/National_Health_Insurance_Scheme_(Ghana) "National Health Insurance Scheme (Ghana)") (NHIS), which is strictly designated for [Ghanaian nationals](/wiki/Ghanaian_people "Ghanaian people").[[206]](#cite_note-207) Health care is variable throughout Ghana and in 2012, more than 12 million Ghanaian nationals were covered by the NHIS.[[207]](#cite_note-Ghana:_National_Health_Insurance_Scheme_(NHIS)-208) Urban centres are well served and contain most of the hospitals, clinics, and pharmacies. There are more than 200 hospitals, and Ghana is a destination for [medical tourism](/wiki/Medical_tourism "Medical tourism").[[208]](#cite_note-209) In 2010, there were 0.1 physicians per 1,000 people and as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit), 0.9 hospital beds per 1,000 people.[[178]](#cite_note-databank.worldbank.org-179) In 2010, 5.2% of Ghana's GDP was spent on health.[[209]](#cite_note-Field_Listing:_Health_expenditures-210) In 2020, the WHO announced Ghana became the second country in the WHO African Region to attain regulatory system "maturity level 3", the second-highest in the four-tiered WHO classification of [National medicines regulatory systems](/w/index.php?title=National_medicines_regulatory_systems&action=edit&redlink=1 "National medicines regulatory systems (page does not exist)").[[210]](#cite_note-211)

Life expectancy at birth in 2021 was 68.6 for a female and 63.7 for a male.[[211]](#cite_note-212) In 2013, [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") was to 39 per 1,000 live births.[[212]](#cite_note-Listing:_Infant_mortality_rate-213) Sources vary on life expectancy at birth; the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) estimated 62 years for men and 64 years for women born in 2016.[[213]](#cite_note-214) The fertility rate declined from 3.99 (2000) to 3.28 (2010) with 2.78 in urban region and 3.94 in rural region.[[179]](#cite_note-statsghana.gov.gh-180) The United Nations reports a fertility decline from 6.95 (1970) to 4.82 (2000) to 3.93 live births per woman in 2017.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit), the [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") prevalence was estimated at 1.40% among adults aged 15–49.[[215]](#cite_note-216)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Ghana](/wiki/Education_in_Ghana "Education in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Wikimedia_Outreach_in_Ghana_6.jpeg/250px-Wikimedia_Outreach_in_Ghana_6.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Wikimedia_Outreach_in_Ghana_6.jpeg)

Education system's implementation of [information and communications technology](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology") at the [University of Ghana](/wiki/University_of_Ghana "University of Ghana")

The education system is divided into three parts: basic education, secondary cycle, and tertiary education. "Basic education" lasts 11 years (ages 4‒15).[[216]](#cite_note-GEScurr-217) It is divided into kindergarten (two years), primary school (two modules of three years) and junior high (three years). Junior high school ends with the [Basic Education Certificate Examination](/wiki/Basic_Education_Certificate_Examination "Basic Education Certificate Examination").[[216]](#cite_note-GEScurr-217)[[217]](#cite_note-GEScurr2-218) Once certified, the pupil can proceed to the secondary cycle.[[218]](#cite_note-BECE-219) Hence, the pupil has the choice between general education (offered by the senior high school) and vocational education (offered by the technical senior high school or the technical and vocational institutes). Senior high school lasts three years and leads to the [West African Senior School Certificate Examination](/wiki/West_African_Senior_School_Certificate_Examination "West African Senior School Certificate Examination"), which is a prerequisite for enrollment in a university bachelor's degree programme.[[219]](#cite_note-nab.gov.gh-220): 7 Polytechnics are open to vocational students.[[220]](#cite_note-UNEVOC-221)

A bachelor's degree requires four years of study. It can be followed by a one- or two-year master's degree programme, which can be followed by a PhD programme of at least three years.[[219]](#cite_note-nab.gov.gh-220): 9 A polytechnic programme lasts two or three years.[[220]](#cite_note-UNEVOC-221) Ghana possesses colleges of education.[[221]](#cite_note-222) Some of the universities are the [University of Ghana](/wiki/University_of_Ghana "University of Ghana"), [Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology"), and [University of Cape Coast](/wiki/University_of_Cape_Coast "University of Cape Coast").[[222]](#cite_note-223)

There are more than 95% of children in school.[[223]](#cite_note-UNICEF_–_Basic_Education_and_Gender_Equality-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225) The literacy rate of youth ages 15 to 24 years old was 81% in 2010, with males at 82%,[[225]](#cite_note-226) and females at 80%.[[226]](#cite_note-227) The education system annually attracts [foreign students](/wiki/Foreign_student "Foreign student"), particularly in the university sector.[[227]](#cite_note-228)[[228]](#cite_note-229)

Ghana has a [free education](/wiki/Free_education "Free education") six-year primary school education system beginning at age 6.[[229]](#cite_note-Ghana_Lauded_for_Free_Primary_School_Program-230) The government largely funds basic education comprising public primary schools and public junior high schools. Senior high schools were subsidised by the government until September 2017/2018 academic year that senior high education became free.[[230]](#cite_note-231) At the higher education level, the government funds more than 80% of resources provided to public universities, polytechnics and teacher training colleges. As part of the Free Compulsory Universal Basic Education, Fcube, the government supplies all basic education schools with all their textbooks and other educational supplies, like exercise books. Senior high schools are provided with all their textbook requirements by the government. Private schools acquire their educational material from private suppliers.[[231]](#cite_note-KoinzerNikolai2017-232)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Ghana](/wiki/Culture_of_Ghana "Culture of Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Jollof_rice_by_Chef_Phizzle._Native_Delight._04.jpg/250px-Jollof_rice_by_Chef_Phizzle._Native_Delight._04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jollof_rice_by_Chef_Phizzle._Native_Delight._04.jpg)

Jollof rice is one of the most popular Ghanaian dishes and is known globally by many.

### Food and drink

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Food and drink")]

[Ghanaian cuisine](/wiki/Ghanaian_cuisine "Ghanaian cuisine") includes an assortment of soups and stews with varied seafoods; most Ghanaian soups are prepared with vegetables, meat, poultry or fish.[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) Fish is important in the diet with tilapia, roasted and fried [whitebait](/wiki/Whitebait "Whitebait"), smoked fish and crayfish, all being common components of Ghanaian dishes.[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) [Banku](/wiki/Banku_(dish) "Banku (dish)") (akple) is a common starchy food made from ground corn (maize),[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) and cornmeal based staples kɔmi ([kenkey](/wiki/Kenkey "Kenkey")) and banku (akple) are usually accompanied by some form of fried fish (chinam) or grilled tilapia and a very spicy condiment made from raw red and green chillies, onions and tomatoes (pepper sauce).[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) Banku and tilapia is a combo served in most restaurants.[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) [Fufu](/wiki/Fufu "Fufu") is the most common exported Ghanaian dish and is a delicacy across the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora").[[232]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_cuisine,_banku,_okra_and_soup-233) Rice is an established staple meal across the country, with various rice-based dishes serving as breakfast, lunch and dinner, the main variants are waakye, plain rice and stew (either kontomire or tomato gravy), fried rice and [jollof rice](/wiki/Jollof_rice "Jollof rice").[[233]](#cite_note-234)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Literature")]

This section is an excerpt from [Ghanaian literature](/wiki/Ghanaian_literature "Ghanaian literature").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ghanaian_literature&action=edit)]

[Ghanaian literature](/wiki/Ghanaian_literature "Ghanaian literature") is literature produced by authors from Ghana or in the [Ghanaian diaspora](/wiki/Ghanaians "Ghanaians"). It starts with a long oral tradition, was influenced heavily by western literature during colonial rule, and became prominent with a post-colonial nationalist tradition in the mid-20th century.[[234]](#cite_note-235)[[235]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_literature_:0-236)[[236]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_literature_:1-237) The current literary community continues with a diverse network of voices both within and outside the country, including in film, theatre, and modern digital formats such as blogging.[[235]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_literature_:0-236)[[236]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_literature_:1-237)

The most prominent authors are novelists [J. E. Casely Hayford](/wiki/J._E._Casely_Hayford "J. E. Casely Hayford"), [Ayi Kwei Armah](/wiki/Ayi_Kwei_Armah "Ayi Kwei Armah"), and [Nii Ayikwei Parkes](/wiki/Nii_Ayikwei_Parkes "Nii Ayikwei Parkes"), who gained international acclaim with the books *[Ethiopia Unbound](/wiki/Ethiopia_Unbound "Ethiopia Unbound")* (1911), *[The Beautyful Ones Are Not Yet Born](/wiki/The_Beautyful_Ones_Are_Not_Yet_Born "The Beautyful Ones Are Not Yet Born")* (1968) and *Tail of the Blue Bird* (2009), respectively.[[237]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_literature_LIT-238) In addition to novels, other literary arts and genres such as theatre and poetry have also had a very good development and support at the national level with prominent playwrights, poets and historians [Joe de Graft](/wiki/Joe_de_Graft "Joe de Graft") and [Efua Sutherland](/wiki/Efua_Sutherland "Efua Sutherland").

The Ghanaian national literature radio programme and accompanying publication *[Voices of Ghana](/wiki/Voices_of_Ghana "Voices of Ghana")* (1955–1957) was one of the earliest on the African continent, and helped establish the scope of the contemporary literary tradition in Ghana.[[238]](#cite_note-239) Scholarship of Anglophone Africa sometimes favours literatures from other geographies, such as the [literature of Nigeria](/wiki/Nigerian_literature "Nigerian literature").[[239]](#cite_note-240)

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Clothing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Gyaman_Adinkra_Symbols.jpg/250px-Gyaman_Adinkra_Symbols.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gyaman_Adinkra_Symbols.jpg)

[Adinkra symbols](/wiki/Adinkra_symbols "Adinkra symbols") by [Robert Sutherland Rattray](/wiki/Robert_Sutherland_Rattray "Robert Sutherland Rattray")

During the 13th century, Ghanaians developed their unique art of *[adinkra](/wiki/Adinkra_symbols "Adinkra symbols")* printing. Hand-printed and hand-embroidered adinkra clothes were made and used exclusively by royalty for devotional ceremonies. Each of the motifs that make up the [corpus](/wiki/Text_corpus "Text corpus") of adinkra symbolism has a name and meaning derived from a proverb, a historical event, human attitude, [ethology](/wiki/Ethology "Ethology"), [plant life-form](/wiki/Plant_life-form "Plant life-form"), or shapes of inanimate and man-made objects. The meanings of the motifs may be categorised into aesthetics, ethics, human relations, and concepts.[[240]](#cite_note-LIT-241) The Adinkra symbols have a decorative function as tattoos but also represent objects that encapsulate evocative messages that convey traditional wisdom, aspects of life, or the environment. There are many symbols with distinct meanings, often linked with proverbs. In the words of [Anthony Appiah](/wiki/Kwame_Anthony_Appiah "Kwame Anthony Appiah"), they were one of the means in a pre-literate society for "supporting the transmission of a complex and nuanced body of practice and belief".[[241]](#cite_note-242) [Kente cloth](/wiki/Kente_cloth "Kente cloth"), the traditional or national cloth of Ghana, is worn by most southern Ghanaian ethnic groups, especially, the [Akan](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") Along with the *adinkra cloth,* Ghanaians use many cloth fabrics for their traditional attire.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) The different ethnic groups have their own individual cloth. The most well known is the Kente cloth.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Kente is a very important national costume and clothing, and these clothes are used to make traditional and modern Kente attire.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Different symbols and different colours mean different things.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Kente is the most famous of all the Ghanaian clothes.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Kente is a ceremonial cloth hand-woven on a horizontal [treadle loom](/wiki/Loom "Loom") and strips measuring about 4 inches wide are sewn together into larger pieces of cloths.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Cloths come in various colours, sizes and designs and are worn during very important social and religious occasions.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) In a cultural context, kente is more important than just a cloth as it is a visual representation of history and also a form of written language through weaving.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) The term kente has its roots in the Akan word *kɛntɛn* which means a basket and the first kente weavers used raffia fibres to weave cloths that looked like kenten (a basket); and thus were referred to as *kenten ntoma*; meaning basket cloth.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243) Kente is also woven by the Ewe people (Ewe Kente) in the Volta Region. The main weaving centres are Agortime area and Agbozume. Agbozume has a vibrant kente market attracting patrons from all over west Africa and the diaspora.[[242]](#cite_note-Ghanaian_Kente_Cloth-243)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b4/Comtemporary_Ghanaian_men_fashion.jpg/250px-Comtemporary_Ghanaian_men_fashion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comtemporary_Ghanaian_men_fashion.jpg)

Contemporary Ghanaian men's fashion with Kente and other traditional styles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/17/Comtemporary_Ghanaian_womenswear.jpg/250px-Comtemporary_Ghanaian_womenswear.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comtemporary_Ghanaian_womenswear.jpg)

Contemporary Ghanaian women's fashion with African print/Ankara and other fabrics

Contemporary Ghanaian fashion includes traditional and modern styles and fabrics and has made its way into the African and global fashion scene. The cloth known as [African print fabric](/wiki/African_wax_prints "African wax prints") was created out of Dutch wax textiles. It is believed that in the late 19th century, Dutch ships on their way to Asia stocked with machine-made textiles that mimicked Indonesian [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik") stopped at many West African ports on the way. The fabrics did not do well in Asia. However, in West Africa—mainly Ghana where there was an already established market for cloths and textiles—the client base grew and it was changed to include local and traditional designs, colours and patterns to cater to the taste of the new consumers.[[243]](#cite_note-244) Today outside of Africa it is called "Ankara", and it has a client base well beyond Ghana and Africa as a whole. It is popular among Caribbean peoples and [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"); celebrities such as [Solange Knowles](/wiki/Solange_Knowles "Solange Knowles") and her sister [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") have been seen wearing African print attire.[[244]](#cite_note-245) Many designers from countries in North America and Europe are now using African prints, and they have gained a global interest.[[245]](#cite_note-246) British luxury fashion house [Burberry](/wiki/Burberry "Burberry") created a collection around Ghanaian styles.[[246]](#cite_note-247) Internationally acclaimed Ghanaian-British designer [Ozwald Boateng](/wiki/Ozwald_Boateng "Ozwald Boateng") introduced African print suits in his 2012 collection.[[247]](#cite_note-248)

### Music and dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Music and dance")]

Main articles: [Azonto](/wiki/Azonto "Azonto") and [Kpanlogo](/wiki/Kpanlogo "Kpanlogo")

[Adowa dance](/wiki/Adowa_dance "Adowa dance") form and music performance

Music incorporates types of musical instruments such as the talking drum ensembles, [Akan Drum](/wiki/Akan_Drum "Akan Drum"), [goje](/wiki/Goje "Goje") fiddle and koloko lute, court music, including the Akan [Seperewa](/wiki/Seperewa "Seperewa"), the Akan atumpan, the Ga kpanlogo styles, and log [xylophones](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone") used in asonko music.[[248]](#cite_note-Ghana:_From_Highlife_to_Hiplife-249) Afro-jazz — "the reuniting of African-American jazz with its African roots"[[249]](#cite_note-250) — was created by [Kofi Ghanaba](/wiki/Guy_Warren "Guy Warren").[[250]](#cite_note-251) A form of secular music called [highlife](/wiki/Highlife "Highlife") originated in the 19th and 20th centuries and spread throughout West Africa.[[248]](#cite_note-Ghana:_From_Highlife_to_Hiplife-249)

In the 1990s, a genre of music was created incorporating the influences of highlife, Afro-reggae, [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") and [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music").[[248]](#cite_note-Ghana:_From_Highlife_to_Hiplife-249) This hybrid was called [hiplife](/wiki/Hiplife "Hiplife").[[248]](#cite_note-Ghana:_From_Highlife_to_Hiplife-249)

There are dances for occasions.[[251]](#cite_note-Dance,_Ghana-252) Dances for celebrations include the [Adowa](/wiki/Adowa_dance "Adowa dance"), [Kpanlogo](/wiki/Kpanlogo "Kpanlogo"), [Azonto](/wiki/Azonto "Azonto"), [Klama](/w/index.php?title=Klama&action=edit&redlink=1 "Klama (page does not exist)"), [Agbadza](/wiki/Agbadza "Agbadza"), [Borborbor](/wiki/Borborbor "Borborbor") and [Bamaya](/w/index.php?title=Bamaya&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bamaya (page does not exist)").[[251]](#cite_note-Dance,_Ghana-252) The Nana Otafrija Pallbearing Services, also known as the [Dancing Pallbearers](/wiki/Dancing_Pallbearers "Dancing Pallbearers"), were featured in a BBC feature story in 2017, and footage from the story became part of an Internet meme in the wake of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[252]](#cite_note-253)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Mass media in Ghana](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Ghana "Mass media in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Ghana_Trustworthiness_of_Media.jpg/250px-Ghana_Trustworthiness_of_Media.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghana_Trustworthiness_of_Media.jpg)

Mass media, news and information provided by television

Chapter 12 of the 1992 Constitution of Ghana guarantees [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press") and independence of the media, while Chapter 2 prohibits censorship.[[253]](#cite_note-gov-254) Post-independence, private outlets closed during the military governments, and media laws prevented criticism of government.[[254]](#cite_note-Anokwa-255) Press freedoms were restored in 1992, and after the election in 2000 of Kufuor, the tensions between the private media and government decreased. Kufuor supported press freedom and repealed a [libel](/wiki/Defamation "Defamation") law, and maintained that the media had to act responsibly.[[255]](#cite_note-pr-256) The media have been described as "one of the most unfettered" in Africa.[[256]](#cite_note-bbc-257)

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Further information: [Architecture of Ghana](/wiki/Architecture_of_Ghana "Architecture of Ghana")

See also: [Ghana's material cultural heritage](/wiki/Ghana%27s_material_cultural_heritage "Ghana's material cultural heritage") and [Ghanaian museums](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Ghana "List of museums in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/A_drone_footage_of_Accra_central%2C_Ghana.jpg/250px-A_drone_footage_of_Accra_central%2C_Ghana.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_drone_footage_of_Accra_central,_Ghana.jpg)

[High-rise buildings](/wiki/High-rise_building "High-rise building") in [Accra](/wiki/Accra "Accra"), the capital

There are two types of construction: the series of adjacent buildings in an enclosure around a common, and the round huts with grass roof.[[257]](#cite_note-ARC-258) The round huts with grass roof architecture are situated in the northern regions, while the series of adjacent buildings are in the southern regions. [Postmodern architecture](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture "Postmodern architecture") and [high-tech architecture](/wiki/High-tech_architecture "High-tech architecture") buildings are in the southern regions, while heritage sites are evident in the more than 30 forts and castles in the country, such as [Fort William](/wiki/Fort_William,_Ghana "Fort William, Ghana") and [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam,_Ghana "Fort Amsterdam, Ghana"). Ghana has museums that are situated inside castles, and two are situated inside a fort.[[258]](#cite_note-Museums_and_Monuments_Board-259) The [Military Museum](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Museum_(Ghana) "Armed Forces Museum (Ghana)") and the [National Museum](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Ghana "National Museum of Ghana") organise temporary exhibitions.[[258]](#cite_note-Museums_and_Monuments_Board-259)

Ghana has museums that allow an in-depth look at specific regions, with a number of museums providing insight into the traditions and history of the geographical areas.[[258]](#cite_note-Museums_and_Monuments_Board-259) The [Cape Coast Castle](/wiki/Cape_Coast_Castle "Cape Coast Castle") Museum and St. George's Castle ([Elmina Castle](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle")) Museum offer guided tours. The [Museum of Science and Technology](/wiki/Kwame_Nkrumah_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology") provides its visitors with a look into the domain of scientific development.[[258]](#cite_note-Museums_and_Monuments_Board-259)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghana&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in Ghana](/wiki/Sports_in_Ghana "Sports in Ghana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/2010_Opening_Ceremony_-_Ghana_entering.jpg/250px-2010_Opening_Ceremony_-_Ghana_entering.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010_Opening_Ceremony_-_Ghana_entering.jpg)

Ghanaian [winter sports](/wiki/Winter_sport "Winter sport") Olympic team at the [opening ceremony](/wiki/Opening_ceremony "Opening ceremony") of the 2010 [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics")

[Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the top spectator sport in Ghana.[[259]](#cite_note-Ghana_thrilled_by_historic_title-260) Ghana has won the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") four times, the [FIFA U-20 World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-20_World_Cup "FIFA U-20 World Cup") once, and has participated in five [FIFA World Cups](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") (2006, 2010, 2014, 2022, and 2026) and has also won the FIFA U-17 World Cup twice.[[259]](#cite_note-Ghana_thrilled_by_historic_title-260) The [International Federation of Football History and Statistics](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Football_History_%26_Statistics "International Federation of Football History & Statistics") crowned Asante Kotoko SC as the [African club of the 20th century](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Football_History_%26_Statistics#Continental_Clubs_of_the_20th_century "International Federation of Football History & Statistics").[[260]](#cite_note-Africa's_club_of_the_Century-261)

Ghana competes in the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), sending athletes in every edition since [1954](/wiki/1954_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1954 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") (except for the [1986 games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games")). Ghana has won 57 medals at the Commonwealth Games, including 15 gold, with all but one of their medals coming in athletics and boxing. The country has also produced a number of boxers, including [Azumah Nelson](/wiki/Azumah_Nelson "Azumah Nelson") a three-time world champion,[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-Top_5_Ghanaian_Boxers-263) [Nana Yaw Konadu](/wiki/Nana_Konadu "Nana Konadu") also a three-time world champion,[[262]](#cite_note-Top_5_Ghanaian_Boxers-263) [Ike Quartey](/wiki/Ike_Quartey "Ike Quartey"),[[262]](#cite_note-Top_5_Ghanaian_Boxers-263) and [Joshua Clottey](/wiki/Joshua_Clottey "Joshua Clottey").[[262]](#cite_note-Top_5_Ghanaian_Boxers-263)
